question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an AccountInput with an alias field that violates the @Pattern(regexp: '^[A-Z2-7]+$') directive and, because OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL silently returns null instead of rejecting the request, cause the server to resolve a different account's balance than intended, leading to direct loss of funds by displaying incorrect balance data to a user making a financial decision?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an AccountInput where the evmAddress field fails the @Pattern(regexp: '^(0x)?[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$') validation and, due to OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL returning null silently, cause the resolver to fall through to an unintended code path that resolves a wrong account, resulting in a user being shown another account's balance and losing funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an id field in AccountInput that fails the @Pattern(regexp: '^[a-zA-Z0-9+/=]+$') directive and, because OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL does not reject the request but returns null, cause the account query to silently resolve to null and hide a real account balance from a user, leading to a missed financial obligation or incorrect fund transfer?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RETURN_NULL validation error strategy in ValidationSchemaWiring to bypass all @Pattern and @Min directive enforcement on AccountInput fields, effectively submitting arbitrary unvalidated input that reaches the EntityService layer and causes incorrect account resolution, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query with exactly 200 complexity units (the MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation threshold) that causes the server to execute a maximally expensive query, exhausting backend database resources and causing legitimate balance queries from other users to time out or return stale data, leading to incorrect fund transfer decisions?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a query that stays at or just below the MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation(200) threshold while requesting the balance field with multiple HbarUnit arguments across many aliased fields, causing the server to return multiple conflicting balance values in a single response that a client application misinterprets, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query at exactly depth 10 (the MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation threshold) that traverses account -> autoRenewAccount -> stakedAccount -> autoRenewAccount chains, causing the server to resolve deeply nested account balances and return a balance for a different account than the one the user queried, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation(10) counts structural depth but not fragment spread depth, allowing them to use inline fragments to exceed the effective depth limit and resolve deeply nested account objects whose balances are returned incorrectly, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the PreparsedDocumentProvider cache by submitting a query string that is identical to a legitimate query but with different variable values, causing the cache to return a previously parsed document that was validated against different input constraints, bypassing per-request validation and resolving the wrong account balance?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user poison the CachedPreparsedDocumentProvider cache (keyed on executionInput.getQuery()) by submitting a malformed query that caches a PreparsedDocumentEntry with a validation error, causing all subsequent users who send the same query string to receive null balance results instead of real balances, leading to direct loss of funds through incorrect financial decisions?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the registration of ExtendedScalars.Object in the RuntimeWiringConfigurer to inject an arbitrarily complex nested object as the key field of an Account response, causing a client application to misparse the key structure and incorrectly authorize a fund transfer that should have been rejected?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query that requests the key field (typed as ExtendedScalars.Object) for an account and receive an arbitrarily large nested JSON object that, when deserialized by a downstream client, causes a buffer overflow or incorrect key comparison, leading to unauthorized fund transfers?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GraphQlDuration.INSTANCE scalar registered in runtimeWiringConfigurer() by supplying an ISO 8601 duration string with an extremely large value (e.g., P999999999Y) that, when parsed by Duration.parse() without bounds checking, causes an arithmetic overflow in autoRenewPeriod calculations, leading to incorrect account expiry and loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a GraphQlTimestamp.INSTANCE scalar value with a timestamp far in the future or past (e.g., '+999999999-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z') that, when parsed by Instant.parse() without range validation, causes incorrect time-based account state resolution and returns a stale or wrong balance, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mismatch between the Jackson maxStringLength(11000) limit and the GraphQL parser maxCharacters(10000) limit by crafting a JSON request body with a string field of length between 10001 and 11000 characters that passes Jackson parsing but is then processed by the GraphQL layer with unexpected behavior, causing incorrect account resolution and direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the asymmetry between StreamReadConstraints maxNestingDepth(10) and StreamWriteConstraints maxNestingDepth(100) in jacksonCustomizer() to craft a request that is accepted at depth 10 but whose serialized response expands to depth 100, causing a client to receive a deeply nested balance object that is misinterpreted, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user send a JSON request body with exactly 100 tokens (the maxTokenCount limit) structured to maximize the complexity of the GraphQL variables object, bypassing the Jackson token limit while still triggering expensive server-side processing that causes balance query results to be returned incorrectly or incompletely?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a JSON request with a field name of exactly 100 characters (the maxNameLength limit) that collides with an internal field name used by the GraphQL execution engine, causing incorrect variable binding and resolving the wrong account's balance, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a JSON body with a number field of exactly 19 digits (the maxNumberLength limit) that overflows a Long when parsed, causing the EntityId shard/realm/num fields to wrap around to a different account's identifier and resolve the wrong account balance, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an AccountInput with all four fields (alias, entityId, evmAddress, id) set to null and, because OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL returns null for each failed validation rather than throwing an error, bypass the validateOneOf() check in AccountController and cause an unhandled IllegalStateException that leaks internal state information useful for targeting fund theft?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL behavior to submit an AccountInput where the alias field contains lowercase characters (violating '^[A-Z2-7]+$'), causing the alias to be treated as null by the validation layer, which then falls through to a different lookup path (entityId, evmAddress, or id) that resolves a completely different account's balance?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a batch of GraphQL queries that each have complexity just below 200 and submit them concurrently, overwhelming the server's thread pool and causing race conditions in the CachedPreparsedDocumentProvider that serve one user's parsed query document to another user's execution context, resulting in wrong account balance being returned?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ChainedInstrumentation ordering (MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation first, then MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation) by crafting a query that passes complexity checks but uses recursive fragment spreads to exceed the effective depth, bypassing MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation(10) and causing unbounded account graph traversal that returns incorrect balance data?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user register a query that requests the balance field with unit=GIGABAR and, due to the integer division in convertCurrency() (tinybars / 100_000_000_000_000_000L), cause a truncation that rounds a large balance down to zero, causing a user to believe an account has no funds and make incorrect financial decisions leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user query the pendingReward field with unit=GIGABAR and exploit the integer division truncation in convertCurrency() to receive a value of 0 for a non-zero pending reward, causing them to make a staking decision based on incorrect reward data and lose expected staking income?
